title: Zimtsterne,German cinnamon stars
subtitle: Almond-and-cinnamon stars under a meringue glaze
excerpt: The German Christmas biscuit: a soft almond-and-cinnamon dough cut into stars and topped with a thin meringue glaze that sets bright white in the oven. Flourless, naturally gluten-free, and built from three core ingredients,ground almonds, sugar, and egg white. They keep for weeks in a tin.
sourceNotes: Zimtsterne appear in German baking compilations from the late nineteenth century onward, including Henriette Davidis, Praktisches Kochbuch (1844 and many later editions; foundational German cookery, now public domain). The egg-white-sugar-almond ratio (2:3:3 by weight, broadly) is documented across regional German Weihnachtsbäckerei traditions from the Erzgebirge through Switzerland (where the same biscuit is called Zimtstern). The meringue glaze separated from the dough is a twentieth-century refinement.

recipe.batchNotes: The dough doubles cleanly; do it in two trays staggered through the oven rather than crowding.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the dough the day before, wrap tightly, and chill. Bring to room temperature for 20 minutes before rolling.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 120°C. A low temperature keeps the white glaze pale and prevents the almonds from oiling out.

recipeTools.notes: For spreading the glaze on each star.

glossaryTerms.definition: An uncooked icing made by whisking egg whites with icing sugar until the mixture is glossy and holds a soft peak. It sits between a royal icing and an Italian meringue,thicker than the first, less stable than the second. Spread thin onto biscuit dough, it sets in the oven to a bright matt white. Whisk just to soft-peak; over-whipped, it cracks as the biscuits cool.

content.text: Zimtsterne are German almond biscuits cut into stars and crowned with a thin white meringue glaze. They are flourless and naturally gluten-free, built from ground almonds, sugar, egg white, and a generous quantity of cinnamon. The dough is soft and clay-like rather than firm; the glaze is the structural top layer that gives them their bright matt finish. Plan around 35 minutes of active work and one fridge rest.

Two technical points to keep in mind. The dough is sticky,handle it cool and rest it cold between rolling. The glaze must spread thin, like a coat of dense paint; thick glaze cracks as the biscuits cool.

items.prepNote: from large eggs, at room temperature
ground almonds, skin on or off

content.text: Whisk the egg whites in a clean grease-free bowl until they hold soft peaks. Add the sifted icing sugar a tablespoon at a time, whisking between additions, until the meringue is glossy and holds a firm peak. The texture should be like a thick royal icing,spreadable, but standing tall.
into a small bowl and set aside, covered, in the fridge,this is the glaze for the tops.

Combine the ground almonds, cinnamon, and lemon zest in a separate bowl. Tip into the remaining meringue and fold with a spatula until the mixture comes together into a soft, slightly sticky dough. It will feel like cool damp clay. Wrap and chill for 1 hour.

Heat the oven to 140°C conventional (120°C fan) and line two baking trays with paper. Dust the worktop with icing sugar,not flour,and tip the dough out. Roll between two sheets of baking paper to about 8 mm thick. Cut stars with a 5 cm cutter, dipping the edge into icing sugar between cuts. Lift onto the trays, spaced 2 cm apart. Re-roll the offcuts once; a second re-roll dries the dough out.

Stir the reserved meringue with a teaspoon,it will have stiffened slightly. Spread a thin layer over the top of each star with a small spatula, leaving 2 mm clear around the edge so it does not run. The glaze should sit as a flat coat, not a dome.

Bake for 18 to 20 minutes, until the glaze is set, matt, and bright white and the bases are pale gold. Domestic ovens drift; an oven thermometer takes the guessing out,too high and the glaze yellows. Cool on the tray for 5 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack. Store in an airtight tin between layers of baking paper; they keep crisp for at least 3 weeks.
